payload.text:
Build a brand new app from scratch called RoninClip. Keep the same visual style and navigation concept as my screenshots: left sidebar with tabs Extraction, Batch Queue, Protocols, System, dark gritty UI, red accents, monospaced micro text in places. Do not simplify the UI into generic buttons. Keep it fast and minimal.

Purpose
This app is a local personal tool to extract chapter titles and chapter text from web pages that I have permission to access, and export them to plain text files in bulk. Do not implement paywall bypass, login scraping, CAPTCHA solving, DRM bypass, or anti bot evasion. Respect rate limits and handle blocks by stopping with a clear error.

Core problem to solve
My target chapter pages follow a structure like this:
The chapter content container is:
#chr-content
Inside it, the first paragraph is the chapter title:
#chr-content > p:first-child
The chapter body is the remaining p elements under #chr-content
There are injected ad blocks inside the chapter flow like:
div id="pf-15550-1", div id="pf-15551-1"
There are also many empty p tags.
Therefore extraction must:

1. Use a protocol template matched by hostname to choose selectors
2. Extract title from #chr-content > p:first-child
3. Extract text from remaining non empty p tags under #chr-content
4. Remove any div whose id starts with pf- and other unwanted nodes
5. Output clean plain text with paragraphs separated by blank lines
6. Avoid duplicating the title in the body

Architecture
Frontend: Vite + React + TypeScript + Tailwind. Use HashRouter. Same sidebar layout and pages.
Backend: Node + Express + TypeScript, runs on port 8787. Use tsx to run TypeScript reliably.
Parsing: Cheerio for HTML parsing. Optional Playwright fallback only if a protocol specifies a waitForSelector. No stealth plugins.
Storage: JSON files in a local data folder: settings.json and templates.json plus a batch export folder.

Data models
ProtocolTemplate
id: string
domain: string (always stored as normalized hostname, never full URL)
titleSelector: string
contentSelector: string
removalSelectors: string[] (optional)
createdAt: number
updatedAt: number

AppSettings
concurrency: number
rateLimitPerMinute: number
outputDir: string
filenamePattern: string

Domain normalization and matching
Implement normalizeHost(input):
If input is a URL, parse hostname
Else treat as hostname
Lowercase
Strip leading [www](http://www).
Return hostname

When saving a protocol, always store normalizeHost(domainInput).
When extracting, compute host = normalizeHost(chapterUrl).
Find protocol where:
host equals protocol.domain
or host endsWith("." + protocol.domain)
If none found, use auto extract heuristics, but show a warning that no protocol matched.

Extraction algorithm when protocol exists
Given html, protocol:
Load cheerio
root = first match of protocol.contentSelector
If no match, return error: content selector matched 0 elements
Within root:
Remove any nodes matching each selector in protocol.removalSelectors
Additionally always remove:
div[id^="pf-"], script, style, iframe, noscript
title = text of first match of protocol.titleSelector
Collect paragraphs:
paras = all p within root, map to trimmed text, filter non empty
If paras[0] equals title, remove paras[0]
body = paras joined with blank line
Return { title, body }

If no p tags found, fallback to root.text() normalized.

Text normalization
Trim whitespace, normalize CRLF to LF, collapse 3+ newlines to 2, remove duplicate blank lines.
Do not output HTML tags in the final text.

Features

1. Extraction page
   Input URL
   Execute button
   Shows extracted title and a scrollable body preview
   Buttons: Copy Title, Copy Body, Download TXT

2. Batch Queue page
   Paste many URLs (one per line)
   Add to queue
   Start, Pause, Clear
   Show queue table with status: pending, running, ok, failed
   Show errors per row
   On completion: Download ZIP of all chapters as TXT plus a manifest.json

Batch behavior
Use concurrency and rateLimit from settings.
Polite delays, exponential backoff on 429 and 503.
Stop and mark failed if blocked.

3. Protocols page
   Show stored domains list with a small plain text preview snippet (not HTML).
   Select a protocol to edit.
   Initialize new protocol.
   Fields:
   Target domain input (accept URL or hostname, store normalized hostname)
   Title selector
   Content selector
   Removal selectors CSV
   Diagnostics:
   Test URL input
   Preview button that returns counts:
   Title matches count
   Content matches count
   Paragraph count extracted
   First 120 chars of title and body
   Also show the computed normalized hostname and which protocol matched

4. System page
   Show backend connection status
   Edit concurrency, rate limit, output folder, filename pattern
   Save settings
   Make sure settings always loads without crashing and shows clear errors.

Backend endpoints
GET /api/health
GET /api/settings
POST /api/settings
GET /api/templates
POST /api/templates (upsert)
DELETE /api/templates/:id
POST /api/extract { url }
POST /api/protocol/diagnostics { url, domain, titleSelector, contentSelector, removalSelectors }
POST /api/batch/add { urls }
POST /api/batch/start
POST /api/batch/stop
POST /api/batch/clear
GET /api/batch/status
GET /api/batch/download

Important engineering requirements
Always return JSON, even on errors: { error: message }
Do not let the server crash on missing files. Create defaults if templates.json or settings.json are missing or empty.
Use type only imports for TypeScript types so Node ESM does not error.
Avoid port conflicts: allow PORT env override, default 8787.

Frontend networking
Do not rely on Vite proxy. Use API_BASE:
In dev, API_BASE = [http://localhost:8787](http://localhost:8787)
In production, API_BASE = empty string.
All API calls must go through a safe fetchJson helper that reads res.text() first and throws readable errors.

Deliverables
Provide full source code for the new app:
package.json, vite config, server files, components, types, and README.
README must include Windows commands:
npm install
npm run dev
How to add a protocol for a site with #chr-content layout
How to export batch chapters.

Acceptance tests

1. With a saved protocol domain novelbin.com and selectors:
   titleSelector #chr-content > p:first-child
   contentSelector #chr-content
   removalSelectors div[id^="pf-"]
   Extraction must produce title and body with no HTML tags and no duplicate title.
2. Batch of 50 URLs processes with concurrency 2 and rate limit 30 per minute and outputs zip successfully.

**Designing RoninClip's Interface**

I'm currently focused on the interface design for RoninClip.  It needs to be a fresh start, while keeping the old ChapterClipper's look and feel. I'm prioritizing ease of use for local chapter extraction.  I'm exploring the best way to present functionalities in this new application.


**Developing Core Logic**

I've been solidifying the core logic, specifically the extraction algorithm targeting `#chr-content`. Title extraction is now defined as the first child, and I'm removing advertisement components using `pf-` pattern exclusion. I've also incorporated a batch queue system with rate limits and concurrency to manage multiple extraction requests efficiently. Furthermore, I've decided to store all of the application settings and custom templates locally.


**Defining App Architecture**

I'm now focusing on defining the complete app architecture. I've fleshed out the tech stack (Express, Playwright, etc.) and configured the `vite.config.ts` proxy for development. Key data structures are defined in `types.ts`, and I've crafted a robust `fetchJson` helper. The server side is taking shape with storage, domain normalization, and extraction logic using Cheerio. The batch queue is set up with rate limiting, and Express routes are ready.


**Building Extraction Pipeline**

Currently, I'm integrating the refined extraction algorithm and protocol matching into the codebase. The server-side logic is taking shape, addressing strict rules for content selection, removal of unwanted elements, and title/body extraction. I'm also ensuring robust protocol matching to correctly identify domains.


**Defining RoninClip's Structure**

I'm now diving into the file structure for RoninClip, ensuring everything's in place. I've mapped out the key files, including React components, server-side modules, and configuration files. I'm focusing on preserving the Tailwind colors from the provided styles and incorporating the `Sword` icon for the brand. Additionally, I'm integrating a POST endpoint to handle protocol diagnostics, allowing the display of title/body character counts. Furthermore, I've started the implementation for batch ZIP generation with a corresponding manifest.json for proper data organization.


**Crafting XML Generation**

I'm now integrating the XML assembly logic. The goal is to produce valid XML within the generated ZIP archive, ensuring compatibility.

# RoninClip

A local personal tool to extract web chapters to plain text.

## Setup

1. **Install Dependencies:**
   ```bash
   npm install
   ```
2. **Install Browsers (for Playwright fallback):**
   ```bash
   npx playwright install chromium
   ```

## Running the App

Start both backend (port 8787) and frontend (port 5173):
```bash
npm run dev
```
Open [http://localhost:5173](http://localhost:5173).

## Features

### Protocols
RoninClip uses "Protocols" to understand how to scrape specific sites.
1. Go to **Protocols**.
2. Initialize New.
3. Enter Domain (e.g., `novelbin.com`).
4. Enter Selectors (e.g., Title: `h3`, Content: `#chr-content`).
5. Use the **Diagnostics** tool to test a live URL and see if it captures the correct title and paragraph count.

### Batch
1. Go to **Batch Queue**.
2. Paste a list of chapter URLs.
3. Click **Engage**.
4. Once finished, click **Archive** to download a ZIP file.

### System Settings
Adjust concurrency and rate limits in **System** to respect server load.

I'll provide clear instructions to create a new protocol, suggest specific selectors for the title and content, and explain how to test and run the extraction process, focusing on user-friendliness and addressing the user's specific query about novelbin.com.


**Developing Content Selectors**

I'm focusing now on specific CSS selectors that remove unwanted elements. I've identified `.chr-nav`, `div[id^="pf-"]`, `script`, and `style` as key targets for removal. The user will be given these values. This will help refine the content extraction.

To use RoninClip with **novelbin.com**, you need to teach the app how to read that specific site by creating a **Protocol**.

Here is the step-by-step guide:

### 1. Create the Protocol
1. Open the app and click on **Protocols** in the left sidebar.
2. Click the **Initialize New** button (top right).
3. Fill in the configuration form with these settings (based on standard NovelBin structure):
   * **Domain**: `novelbin.com`
   * **Title Selector**: `h3` 
     *(The chapter title is usually inside an `<h3>` tag)*
   * **Content Selector**: `#chr-content`
     *(The main text is usually inside a div with the id `chr-content`)*
   * **Removal Selectors**: `.ads, .google-auto-placed`
     *(Optional: Helps clean up specific ad elements if they appear inside the text)*
4. Click **Save Protocol**.

### 2. Verify with Diagnostics (Optional but Recommended)
1. While still in the **Protocols** tab with your new protocol selected.
2. Paste a specific chapter URL into the **Test URL** box in the Diagnostics panel.
   * *Example:* `https://novelbin.com/b/shadow-slave/chapter-1`
3. Click **Test**.
4. Look at the results:
   * **TITLE MATCH**: Should be green (1 match).
   * **CONTENT MATCH**: Should be green (1 match).
   * **PREVIEWS**: Read the snippet to ensure it looks like actual story text, not menu items.

### 3. Extract Content
Now that the system knows how to handle the site, you can extract chapters.

**For a Single Chapter:**
1. Go to **Extraction**.
2. Paste the chapter URL.
3. Click **Execute**.
4. You can read it on screen or click **TXT** to download.

**For Multiple Chapters (Batch):**
1. Go to **Batch Queue**.
2. Paste a list of URLs (one per line).
   * *Tip: You can usually use a browser extension like "Link Klipper" to grab all chapter links from a Table of Contents page.*
3. Click **Engage**.
4. Watch the status change from `WAITING` to `OK`.
5. Once finished, click **Archive** to download a ZIP file containing all your chapters as clean text files.

### Step 2: Push to GitHub

Once the file above is applied, follow these steps in your terminal (VS Code terminal is fine):

1.  **Initialize Git:**
    ```bash
    git init
    ```

2.  **Add your files:**
    ```bash
    git add .
    ```

3.  **Commit the files:**
    ```bash
    git commit -m "Initial commit of RoninClip"
    ```

4.  **Create the Repo on GitHub:**
    *   Go to [github.com/new](https://github.com/new).
    *   Name it `ronin-clip`.
    *   **Do not** check "Add a README" or ".gitignore" (you already have them).
    *   Click **Create repository**.

5.  **Connect and Push:**
    *   Copy the commands GitHub shows you under "...or push an existing repository from the command line". It will look like this:
    ```bash
    git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/ronin-clip.git
    git branch -M main
    git push -u origin main
    ```

### Note on "Online" Usage
If you want to use this app from your phone or another computer without running the terminal commands, you cannot use GitHub. You would need to rent a **VPS** (Virtual Private Server) like DigitalOcean, Hetzner, or Railway that supports **Node.js + Playwright**.

For now, keeping it local and saving your code to GitHub is the best workflow.

Now that I've added the `Dockerfile` and updated the server:

    *   It will take about 3-4 minutes to build.
3.  **Done**: You will get a URL (like `ronin-clip-production.up.railway.app`) that you can share or use from your phone.

*Note: Railway is not free (it has a trial), but it is the most reliable way to run "Headless Chrome" in the cloud.*

### The Breakdown

1.  **`FROM mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.42.1-jammy`**
    *   **Why:** This is the most critical line. Standard Node.js images (like `node:20`) are missing the system-level dependencies (libraries for graphics, fonts, audio) required to run a "Headless" browser.
    *   If you don't use this image, your app will crash with errors like `libgbm.so.1: cannot open shared object file` when it tries to extract a chapter.
    *   We use version `v1.42.1` to match the version of `playwright` in your `package.json` to avoid compatibility issues.

2.  **`ENV PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD=1`**
    *   **Why:** The base image (Step 1) already comes pre-installed with the browsers (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit).
    *   Without this line, when `npm install` runs, Playwright will waste time and bandwidth trying to download the browsers again.

3.  **`WORKDIR /app`**
    *   **Why:** Sets the context folder so we don't pollute the root directory of the container.

4.  **`COPY package*.json ./` and `RUN npm ci`**
    *   **Why:** We copy the package files *before* the rest of the code. Docker caches layers; if you change your source code but not your dependencies, Docker skips the heavy `npm install` step on the next build, making deployments much faster. `npm ci` is used for clean, reproducible builds.

5.  **`COPY . .`**
    *   **Why:** Copies the rest of your application code (React components, server logic) into the container.

6.  **`RUN npm run build`**
    *   **Why:** Your frontend is React (Vite). Browsers cannot understand `.tsx` files directly. This command compiles your React code into static HTML/CSS/JS files located in the `dist/` folder, which your Express server will then serve.

7.  **`EXPOSE 8787`**
    *   **Why:** Documentation for Docker to know which port your application listens on.

8.  **`CMD ["npm", "start"]`**
    *   **Why:** This runs the command defined in your `package.json`. In the previous step, we set `"start": "tsx server/index.ts"`. This starts your Express server, which serves both the API and the React frontend.

Yep. Railway is the right choice for this kind of app because it needs a real Node server, and if you use Playwright it also needs browser dependencies.

Here is a Dockerfile that works well on Railway for a Vite frontend plus Express backend plus Playwright.

Dockerfile

FROM mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:v1.42.1-jammy

ENV PLAYWRIGHT_SKIP_BROWSER_DOWNLOAD=1
ENV NODE_ENV=development

WORKDIR /app

COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

COPY . .

RUN npm run build

ENV NODE_ENV=production

EXPOSE 8787

CMD ["npm","run","start"]

Also add a .dockerignore so Railway builds faster and does not upload junk

Two required app changes so Railway can actually reach it

1. Your server must listen on process.env.PORT and bind to 0.0.0.0

In server/index.ts, your listen should be like this:

const PORT = Number(process.env.PORT) || 8787;
app.listen(PORT, "0.0.0.0", () => {
console.log(`Server running at http://0.0.0.0:${PORT}`);
});

If you only listen on localhost, Railway cannot connect.

2. Your server must serve the built frontend from dist

Add this near the bottom of server/index.ts after API routes:

import path from "node:path";
import { fileURLToPath } from "node:url";

const __filename = fileURLToPath(import.meta.url);
const __dirname = path.dirname(__filename);

const distPath = path.resolve(__dirname, "../dist");
app.use(express.static(distPath));

app.get("*", (req, res) => {
res.sendFile(path.join(distPath, "index.html"));
});

This makes the app one service, one port, which is what Railway wants.

Your package.json must have a start script that runs only the server

If you currently rely on npm run dev and concurrently, do not use that in Docker.

Use something like:

"scripts": {
"dev": "concurrently "npm run server" "vite"",
"server": "tsx server/index.ts",
"build": "vite build",
"start": "node server/index.ts"
}

But if server/index.ts is TypeScript, node cannot run it directly. You have two safe options.

Option A, run TS in production with tsx
Change start to:

"start": "tsx server/index.ts"

Then keep tsx installed (it can stay in devDependencies if npm ci installs dev deps, which the Dockerfile above does).

Option B, compile server to JS and run node
Add a server build and make start run the compiled JS. This is the cleanest long term, but takes a bit more setup.

Railway checklist

1. Commit Dockerfile and .dockerignore to GitHub
2. In Railway, New Project, Deploy from GitHub repo
3. Railway will detect Dockerfile automatically
4. In Railway settings, make sure you do not hardcode a port. Railway sets PORT. Your server must use it.
